coconstruction_stages: Show students three unlabeled descriptions of how decisions get made in democracies and ask them to sort and name the patterns they notice.; Guide students to identify the three model names and list what makes each one distinct from the others.; Have students generate their own examples of each model in action and test whether those examples fit the characteristics they identified.; Co-author level descriptors by asking students what 'knowing this well' looks like at each stage of understanding.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What are the key differences in how power and participation work across these three descriptions?; Which model emphasizes ordinary citizens making decisions, which emphasizes organized groups negotiating, and which emphasizes experts or elites deciding?; Can you find a real-world example where one of these models is at work and explain why it fits that model?; What would someone need to do or understand to move from naming the models to comparing how they actually work?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that clearly embody one model without overlap—for instance, a town hall meeting for participatory, a lobbying coalition for pluralist, and a technocratic policy decision for elite. Avoid examples that blur two models together.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to identify or explain the three models of representative democracy.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name one or two of the models and describe some characteristics, though my explanations may be incomplete or not quite accurate.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify all three models and describe some of their key characteristics on my own, but my explanations are still missing details or are not fully precise.
student_rubric_competent: I can explain the key characteristics of participatory, pluralist, and elite models of representative democracy clearly and accurately.
student_rubric_extending: I can compare and contrast the three models, identify tensions between them, and explain the underlying assumptions that shape each framework.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I name all three models and describe at least two characteristics that make each one different from the others?; Can I explain how each model answers the question 'Who really makes decisions in a democracy?'
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Ask the student to name just one model and describe what role ordinary citizens play in that model.; Provide a simple scenario of a decision being made and ask which model it most closely resembles.
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask the student to name the third model they haven't mentioned yet and describe one way it differs from the first two.; Prompt them to explain what 'pluralist' means by asking who the key decision-makers are in that model.
feedback_moves_developing: Ask the student to add one more specific characteristic to each model that they haven't mentioned yet.; Have them test their understanding by applying each model to a new real-world scenario and explaining which model fits best.
feedback_moves_competent: Ask the student to identify a tension or contradiction between two of the models.; Prompt them to explain what each model assumes about human nature or how democracy should work.

coconstruction_stages: Show students a primary source excerpt from the Convention and ask them to identify what problem or disagreement the delegates were trying to solve.; Have students brainstorm what a 'compromise' means and list examples from their own experience of how two sides reach agreement.; Guide students to name specific compromises from the Convention and record them on a shared chart.; Ask students to describe what each compromise actually changed in the Constitution's structure and how it settled the conflict.; Invite students to reflect on whether some compromises were more important than others and why.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What was the main disagreement between delegates that led to this compromise?; How did this compromise change what the Constitution actually says or how government works?; Which compromises do you think had the biggest impact on the structure of our government, and why?; Can you name a compromise and explain both sides of the conflict it resolved?; What would the Constitution look like if this compromise had not been made?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select one compromise that is straightforward and well-documented in primary sources and one that shows clear structural consequences in the final document; avoid compromises that are ambiguous or require deep historical context beyond the Convention itself.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to identify or describe any constitutional compromises.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name one or two compromises from the Convention but provide only basic or unclear details about how they shaped the Constitution.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify several major compromises on my own but sometimes miss details about their structural outcomes or describe them inconsistently.
student_rubric_competent: I can identify major compromises and clearly explain how each one changed the Constitution's structure and resolved the conflict between delegates.
student_rubric_extending: I can identify compromises and explain how they reflect deeper disagreements about power and ideology, and how they limited or shaped later interpretations of the Constitution.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I name at least three major compromises and describe what each one actually changed in the Constitution?; Can I explain what conflict or disagreement each compromise was meant to solve?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Ask the student to choose one compromise from a provided list and tell you what problem it solved.; Provide a primary source quote about a specific compromise and ask the student to identify what two sides disagreed about.
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask the student to add one more compromise to their list and describe what structural change it made to the government.; Prompt the student to clarify a vague description by asking 'What part of the Constitution did this compromise affect?'
feedback_moves_developing: Ask the student to check their descriptions against the actual text of the Constitution and revise any that are incomplete.; Have the student compare two compromises and explain which one had a bigger impact on how government works and why.
feedback_moves_competent: Ask the student to consider what ideological or philosophical disagreement lay beneath one of their compromises.; Prompt the student to research how one compromise influenced later debates or court decisions about constitutional meaning.

competency_cluster: cluster_07: Federalism and the Distribution of Power
coconstruction_stages: Show students three real constitutional powers and ask them to sort by who holds them: only national, only state, or both.; Guide students to name each category and predict what the names might mean based on the sorting.; Have students draft definitions for exclusive, reserved, and concurrent by examining the powers in each group.; Test definitions against new powers and refine language together until students can apply them consistently.
coconstruction_student_prompts: Which of these powers belong only to the national government, only to states, or to both?; What do you notice about the word 'exclusive'—what does it tell you about who can do that power?; How would you explain to someone why a concurrent power is different from an exclusive power?; Can you think of a power we haven't discussed and predict whether it would be exclusive, reserved, or concurrent?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose one clear example for each power type that students can easily verify in the Constitution or a primary source, and one borderline or debatable case that requires students to apply their definitions rather than memorize.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to identify or name the types of governmental powers.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name exclusive, reserved, or concurrent powers with help, but I mix up what each type means or who holds them.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify and tell the difference between exclusive, reserved, and concurrent powers on my own, but I do not fully explain how each one divides power between national and state governments.
student_rubric_competent: I can identify and distinguish exclusive, reserved, and concurrent powers and clearly explain how each type gives authority to the national government, state governments, or both.
student_rubric_extending: I can apply the three power types to new constitutional situations or connect them to how the three branches of government separate power.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I name all three types of powers and explain what makes each one different?; Can I point to a real power and correctly say whether it belongs to the national government only, the states only, or both?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Ask the student to sort three given powers into groups based on who holds them, then name what they notice about each group.; Provide a definition of one power type and ask the student to find an example of it in a provided list.
feedback_moves_emerging: Have the student explain in their own words what 'exclusive' means, then check their definition against a power that only the national government holds.; Give the student a power and ask them to decide: does only one level of government hold this, or do both, and how do you know?
feedback_moves_developing: Ask the student to complete a sentence: 'Because this power is concurrent, both the national and state governments can ___, which means ___.'; Have the student explain why a specific power is reserved to the states rather than exclusive to the national government.
feedback_moves_competent: Present a novel constitutional scenario and ask the student to identify which power type applies and justify their choice.; Ask the student to explain how understanding exclusive, reserved, and concurrent powers helps them understand the balance of power in the Constitution.

competency_cluster: cluster_07: Federalism and the Distribution of Power
coconstruction_stages: Show students a real federal funding document and ask them to identify what type of money is flowing and who controls it.; Guide students to name the funding mechanisms they notice and sort them into categories based on control and conditions.; Have students compare two mechanisms side-by-side to see which gives states more or less power.; Co-author level descriptors by asking students what 'identifying' and 'comparing' look like at each stage of learning.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What do you notice about who gives the money and who decides how it gets spent?; How is this funding mechanism different from the one we just looked at in terms of state choice?; What would a student who is just starting to understand this look like compared to a student who really gets it?; How could someone compare funding mechanisms across different policy areas like education and transportation?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose funding examples that clearly show different levels of state autonomy: one where the federal government sets strict rules, one where states have significant discretion, and one that blends both. Ensure examples come from different policy domains so students see the pattern across contexts.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet tried to identify or compare federal funding mechanisms.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name one or two federal funding mechanisms with help, but my comparisons are incomplete or mixed up.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify multiple federal funding mechanisms on my own and make some comparisons, but I do not clearly explain how they show power between national and state governments.
student_rubric_competent: I can identify and compare different federal funding mechanisms and explain how each one shows the distribution of power between national and state governments.
student_rubric_extending: I can compare funding mechanisms across different policy areas and evaluate how funding choices shift power between governmental levels in strategic ways.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I name at least three different federal funding mechanisms and describe how each one works?; Can I explain which funding mechanism gives states more power and which gives the federal government more control, and why?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Start with one funding mechanism and ask the student to describe who gives the money and who decides how it is used.; Provide a sentence frame: 'In this funding mechanism, the federal government controls ___ and the state controls ___.'
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask the student to find a second funding mechanism and place it next to the first one to spot one difference in how control works.; Prompt: 'Which of these two gives the state more freedom to spend the money the way it wants?'
feedback_moves_developing: Push the student to explain why the difference in control matters: 'What does it mean for state power when the federal government sets strict rules?'; Have the student apply the same comparison to a funding mechanism in a different policy area to see the pattern.
feedback_moves_competent: Ask the student to predict how a new funding mechanism would shift power and to justify the prediction based on patterns they have seen.; Challenge them to evaluate whether a particular funding design is effective at achieving both federal goals and state flexibility.

competency_cluster: cluster_09: Describing Political Principles and Institutions
coconstruction_stages: Show students a political principle or institution and ask them to name what they see and what it does.; Have students describe how that same principle or institution works in two different real-world scenarios and identify what stays the same and what changes.; Guide students to articulate what 'describing how it operates' means by listing the key details they need to include.; Work together to distinguish between naming something and actually explaining how it works across different contexts.; Co-author level descriptors by asking students what evidence would show they can do this independently versus with help versus not yet.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What is this principle or institution, and what is its main job or purpose?; How does this principle or institution work differently in this scenario compared to that one, and what stays the same in both?; What details do you need to explain to show someone understands how something operates, not just what it is?; How would you know if someone was just naming a principle versus actually describing how it works?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose anchor examples that show the same principle or institution operating in two contrasting contexts so students can see what 'transfer across scenarios' looks like. Select examples where the principle's core function is recognizable but its application differs noticeably.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet tried to describe how a political principle or institution operates.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name a political principle or institution, but my description is incomplete or not quite accurate when I try to explain it in different contexts.
student_rubric_developing: I can independently describe how a political principle or institution operates in one familiar context, but I find it hard to explain how it works in a different scenario.
student_rubric_competent: I can independently describe how political principles, institutions, processes, policies, or behaviors operate accurately across different scenarios and contexts.
student_rubric_extending: I can describe how principles and institutions operate across contexts and explain how they relate to each other or create tensions with one another.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I explain not just what this principle or institution is, but how it actually works in more than one real situation?; Did I describe the same principle or institution in at least two different contexts, and did my explanation stay accurate in both?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Ask the student to identify what principle or institution they are looking at before attempting to describe it.; Provide a familiar context and model one sentence that describes how the principle operates there, then ask the student to try the next sentence.
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask the student to check their description against a second context and identify what they need to add or correct.; Prompt the student to explain not just what the principle does, but how it actually works step-by-step or in practice.
feedback_moves_developing: Introduce a second, less familiar context and ask the student to predict how the principle might operate there before explaining it.; Have the student compare their two descriptions side-by-side to spot what is the same about how the principle operates and what is different about the situation.
feedback_moves_competent: Ask the student to identify a tension or contradiction between how the principle operates in two different contexts.; Invite the student to explain how two related principles or institutions interact or influence each other across contexts.

lt_name: Identifying Arguments and Perspectives in Sources
coconstruction_stages: Show students a short political source and ask them to underline or highlight what they think the author most wants readers to believe.; Facilitate a whole-group discussion where students share what they highlighted and explain why they chose those parts.; Guide students to distinguish between the main argument and other details or claims that support it.; Co-author level descriptors by asking students what it looks like when someone does or does not identify an argument clearly.; Test the rubric together on a second source to refine language and ensure all levels are recognizable.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What is the one big idea or claim the author wants you to believe most?; How do you know that is the main argument and not just a supporting detail?; What would it look like if someone only named the topic but missed the actual argument?; What smaller claims or reasons does the author use to back up the main argument?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose sources that have a clear, single main argument and sources where the argument is buried or mixed with secondary claims; this contrast helps students see the difference between emerging and developing work.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to identify what argument or perspective the author is presenting.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name the topic of the source, but with help I am still working to separate the main argument from other information.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify and state the main argument or perspective on my own, though I may miss some of the finer details or secondary claims.
student_rubric_competent: I can independently identify and pull out the main argument or perspective from a political source accurately and clearly.
student_rubric_extending: I can identify the main argument and explain how it differs from related secondary claims and ideas the author does not state directly.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I state the author's main argument in one or two sentences without listing all the details?; Have I separated the main argument from the supporting reasons or secondary claims?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Ask the student to point to one sentence in the source that tells what the author believes most strongly.; Model thinking aloud: identify the main argument yourself, then ask the student to try the same process on a new source with you.
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask the student to restate the topic they named, then push: what does the author want you to do or believe about that topic?; Provide a sentence frame: the author argues that _____, and use it together to isolate the main claim from supporting details.
feedback_moves_developing: Ask the student to identify one secondary claim or supporting reason and explain how it differs from the main argument.; Prompt: are there any assumptions the author makes that are not stated directly, or any smaller disagreements within the main argument?
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge the student to find an unstated assumption or belief that underlies the main argument.; Ask the student to compare the main argument to a related but different perspective to sharpen their understanding of what makes this argument distinct.

coconstruction_stages: Show students a short political scenario and ask them to list any words or ideas they notice that sound 'political' or 'government-related'.; Guide students to sort their words into four categories: principles, institutions, processes, and policies.; Work together to define what each category means using their own examples from the scenario.; Co-create descriptors for each rubric level by asking students what 'trying but not quite there' versus 'nailing it' looks like.; Test the rubric together on a new scenario to refine language and ensure clarity.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What words or ideas in this scenario remind you of government, rules, or how decisions get made?; Which of these ideas are about values or beliefs, which are organizations, which are steps or actions, and which are rules or plans?; How would you describe the difference between someone who names one concept and someone who finds many and explains how they connect?; What would help you know you've found all the important political ideas in a scenario?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose scenarios that contain a mix of principles, institutions, processes, and policies so students can practice identifying across all four categories; select one scenario where concepts clearly interact or depend on each other to anchor the extending level.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet tried to identify political concepts in the scenario.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name one or two political concepts with help, but I may get their meaning or how they fit the scenario wrong.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify some political concepts on my own, but I might miss some or not fully explain how they connect to the scenario.
student_rubric_competent: I can independently identify multiple political principles, institutions, processes, or policies that are relevant to the scenario.
student_rubric_extending: I can identify concepts and explain how they interact or depend on each other within the scenario.
student_rubric_self_checks: Did I find at least one example from each category: a principle, an institution, a process, and a policy?; Can I explain not just what each concept is, but why it matters to this particular scenario?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Point to one word or phrase in the scenario and ask: 'Is this about government or how decisions are made?'; Offer a sentence starter: 'One political concept I notice is …' and ask the student to complete it.
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask the student to sort their named concepts into the four categories and check if each one truly fits.; Restate what the student said and ask: 'Does that match what actually happens in the scenario?'
feedback_moves_developing: Ask: 'What else in the scenario involves government, rules, or decision-making that you haven't named yet?'; Guide them to pick two concepts they found and explain how one affects the other in the scenario.
feedback_moves_competent: Ask: 'How do these concepts work together or depend on each other to make this scenario happen?'; Prompt them to predict what would change if one of the concepts were removed or changed.

lt_name: Defining Core Governmental Roles and Status
coconstruction_stages: Show students a collection of governmental terms and ask them to sort by familiarity.; Have students work in pairs to write their own definitions of 2–3 terms without looking them up.; Introduce the official definitions and compare them to student definitions to identify what makes a definition complete and accurate.; Build the rubric levels together by asking what 'with support,' 'independently,' and 'nuance' look like in practice.; Co-select anchor examples from student work that match each level.
coconstruction_student_prompts: Which governmental terms do you already know, and which are new to you?; How would you explain this term to someone who has never heard it before?; What details or connections did the official definition include that your definition missed?; What does it mean to explain a term 'independently' versus 'with support'?; What would it look like if someone went beyond just defining a term and showed how it connects to other ideas?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select student work samples that show a clear progression from vague or incomplete attempts to precise, independent explanations that recognize subtle differences between related terms. Prioritize examples that reveal common misconceptions at emerging and developing levels.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to identify or explain governmental terms.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some governmental terms with help, but my explanations are incomplete or not quite right.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify and explain most core governmental terms on my own, but I may miss some details or connections between them.
student_rubric_competent: I can identify and explain core governmental terms related to citizenship and participation accurately and without help.
student_rubric_extending: I can use governmental terminology to show how roles and relationships differ across different political situations or countries.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I define each term in my own words and explain what it means for citizenship or participation?; Have I noticed how different governmental terms relate to or differ from each other?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Ask the student to choose one governmental term and tell you what they already know about it, even if it is just a guess.; Provide a simple definition and ask the student to rephrase it in their own words.
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask the student to add one more detail to their explanation by answering 'Why does this term matter for citizenship?'; Show the student a correct definition and ask them to identify which parts of their explanation were accurate and which need revision.
feedback_moves_developing: Ask the student to compare two related terms and explain how they are different.; Prompt the student to find a real-world example of the term in action and describe how it connects to the definition.
feedback_moves_competent: Ask the student to explain how a governmental term functions differently in two different political systems or contexts.; Invite the student to teach the term to a peer and then reflect on what questions or confusions came up.
